title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Guayusa's Amazonian cloud-forest range
description: Napo Province Ecuador — ancestral Kichwa/Achuar homeland. Guayusa cultivated here for 1,500+ years in the transition zone between Andes and Amazon.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Guayusa's Amazonian cloud-forest range
description: Pastaza Province Ecuador — Achuar + Shuar territory. Wayus Aramu ('guayusa time') ceremony begins pre-dawn.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Guayusa's Amazonian cloud-forest range
description: Peru upper Amazon — guayusa's natural range extends into the northern Peruvian Amazon.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Guayusa's Amazonian cloud-forest range
description: Southern Colombia — Putumayo Department. Obligate cloud-forest understory species; destroy the forest, lose the plant.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut's West African belt
description: Sierra Leone — rainforest belt Kola acuminata + Kola nitida.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut's West African belt
description: Liberia — forest-dependent cultivation.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut's West African belt
description: Côte d'Ivoire forest zone.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut's West African belt
description: Ghana — Akan + Ewe ceremonial use. Kola nut gift opens any important meeting.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut's West African belt
description: Nigeria — Yoruba, Igbo, Hausa. Kola nut ('obi' in Igbo) is central to marriage, diplomacy, and chieftaincy rituals.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut on trans-Saharan caravan routes
description: Nigeria/West African forest — source of kola for the medieval trans-Saharan network.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut on trans-Saharan caravan routes
description: Niamey + Sahel — caravan route staging.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut on trans-Saharan caravan routes
description: Timbuktu — Mali empire gold-salt-kola trade hub; Kola moves from forest to desert to Mediterranean via salt caravans.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut on trans-Saharan caravan routes
description: Marrakesh — North African entry point.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut in the Atlantic slave trade
description: Bight of Benin — enslaved West Africans carried kola tradition to the Americas.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut in the Atlantic slave trade
description: Caribbean — kola cultural practices preserved in diaspora communities in Jamaica, Haiti, Brazil.

title: LAYER 1 — Historical Trade: Kola nut in the Atlantic slave trade
description: Atlanta Georgia — 1886, John Pemberton uses kola nut + coca leaf to invent Coca-Cola. West African commodity becomes global corporate brand.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: Indigenous names preserved
description: Kichwa — 'guayusa' (also spelled wayusa) — this is the English borrowing from Kichwa, not the other way around.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: Indigenous names preserved
description: Achuar Chicham — 'wayus'. The Achuar name co-exists with the Kichwa name.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: Indigenous names preserved
description: Yoruba / Igbo — 'obi' (Igbo), 'obì' (Yoruba). The English 'kola' is from Temne and Mandinka.

title: LAYER 2 — Language Diffusion: Indigenous names preserved
description: English — 'kola' and 'cola'. Survives in 'Coca-Cola' even though modern formula uses only caffeine extract.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Quito Ecuador — specialty export hub. Runa Tea Company + Waykana organize Kichwa smallholder farms for direct-trade export.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Lagos Nigeria — largest kola market on Earth. Kola processing also feeds the global soft-drink extract industry.

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: New York — specialty Amazonian beverage market (guayusa sold as premium energy tea).

title: LAYER 3 — Modern Supply Chain (today)
description: Atlanta — Coca-Cola HQ. Global beverage giant built on West African and South American plant genetics.
